verses.commentary: 'That same day' — continuous from ch 12's family scene. Jesus leaves the house, sits by the sea. The seaside teaching block begins — Matthew's third teaching block, the parables of the kingdom.
Crowds press in. Jesus uses a boat as floating pulpit, the natural amphitheater of shore. Acoustics serve the message.
Parables (parabolai, παραβολαί) — 'thrown alongside.' Stories that compare. The first parable: a sower goes out. Agricultural opening, kingdom themed.
Seed on the path — birds eat it. First soil category: no penetration.
Rocky ground — shallow soil, quick growth. Quick germination as warning, not benefit.
Sun scorches, no root, plants wither. Without depth, vitality cannot survive heat.
Seed among thorns — choked. Competition for soil and light kills the plant slowly.
Good soil yields a hundredfold, sixtyfold, thirtyfold. Even the lower yields are massive — 30x was an exceptional harvest. Kingdom productivity is supernatural where it lands.
'Whoever has ears to hear, let him hear.' Refrain marking parables. Hearing is itself a faculty that requires inner alignment.
Disciples ask: why parables? They notice the shift in pedagogy.
'To you it has been given to know the mysteries (mysteria, μυστήρια) of the kingdom; to them it has not been given.' Revelation has an inside and outside. Parables both disclose and conceal.
'To whoever has, more will be given... whoever does not have, even what he has will be taken.' Spiritual capital compounds in both directions. Receptivity is the multiplier.
'Seeing, they do not see; hearing, they do not hear.' Sensory hardware works; reception hardware does not. The blockage is interior, not informational.
Isaiah 6:9 quoted. The prophet's commission included the message-that-hardens. Same dynamic now in Jesus' ministry — predicted.
'This people's heart has grown dull.' Hearing closed, eyes shut. The defensive closure prevents healing. Repentance would heal them — and they refuse it.
'Blessed are your eyes, for they see.' The disciples are the privileged exception in a generation that does not see.
Many prophets and righteous people longed to see what the disciples see. Salvation-historical position is a gift. They live at the threshold of fulfillment.
Now the interpretation: 'Hear the parable of the sower.' Insider explanation begins.
Path = hearer who does not understand. The evil one snatches the seed away. Lack of comprehension creates the steal-window.
Rocky soil = quick-joy receiver. Initial enthusiasm without depth-formation.
No root — temporary. Tribulation or persecution arrives because of the word, and the hearer 'is offended' (skandalizetai, σκανδαλίζεται). The faith collapses under cost.
Thorns = cares of this age and deceitfulness of riches. The slow strangulation. The seed lives but produces nothing. Worry and wealth are paired killers.
Good soil = hearer who understands. Bears fruit. Comprehension + endurance = productivity. The yields vary — not all hundredfold, but all fruitful.
Another parable: kingdom like a man who sowed good seed.
Enemy sows weeds (zizania, ζιζάνια — darnel, weed that mimics wheat early). Sabotage in the night. Two crops grow together, indistinguishable in youth.
When wheat heads form, weeds appear distinct. Maturity exposes identity. Time reveals what hasty inspection cannot.
Servants notice the problem and ask the master: 'Didn't you sow good seed?' The unexpected mixture demands explanation.
Master: 'An enemy did this.' Diagnosis of sabotage. Servants offer to pull the weeds.
Master refuses — pulling weeds risks uprooting wheat. Aggressive purification damages the genuine. Patience is strategic.
Let both grow until harvest. Then sorting — weeds bundled to burn, wheat into the barn. Mixed church now; sorted kingdom later. Premature judgment forbidden.
Another parable: kingdom like mustard seed. Tiny start.
Smallest of seeds, becomes largest of garden plants — a tree birds rest in (echoing Daniel 4 and Ezekiel 17). Kingdom growth: small origin, vast canopy.
Yeast/leaven (zyme, ζύμη) in three measures of flour (massive amount — feast quantity). Hidden, pervasive, transformative. The kingdom works through penetration, not display.
Public teaching exclusively in parables. Note: insider explanation comes privately. The teaching style is itself a sorting mechanism.
Psalm 78:2 quoted. Parables 'utter things hidden since the foundation of the world.' Mystery-revelation in story form. Fulfillment formula stretches even to teaching method.
Crowd dismissed; Jesus enters the house. Private mode begins. Disciples request the weeds parable's interpretation.
Sower = Son of Man. Christological identification of the parable's protagonist.
Field = world (kosmos, κόσμος, not just Israel). Good seed = sons of the kingdom. Weeds = sons of the evil one. Two seedlines coexist in the world.
Enemy = devil. Harvest = end of the age (synteleia tou aionos, συντέλεια τοῦ αἰῶνος). Harvesters = angels. Eschatological cast laid out.
Weeds burned — the eschatological consummation. Future certain, deferred now.
Son of Man sends angels to weed out 'all causes of sin and all who do evil' (skandala, σκάνδαλα; tous poiountas ten anomian, τοὺς ποιοῦντας τὴν ἀνομίαν). Final purgation.
Furnace of fire — weeping and gnashing of teeth. Matthean refrain (8:12, 13:50, 22:13, 24:51, 25:30). Conscious final loss.
Righteous shine like the sun in the Father's kingdom (compare Daniel 12:3). The resurrection vindication of those who endured the mixed field.
Hidden treasure parable: man finds, hides again, sells everything to buy that field. Joy drives the divestment. The kingdom's value justifies total commitment.
Merchant seeking fine pearls — searching mode versus stumbling-upon mode. Two routes to the kingdom.
Finds one pearl of great value, sells everything to obtain it. Same structural conclusion: total cost for incomparable value. The find is so worth it that the price is no longer a question.
Dragnet parable: net catches all kinds. Initial inclusiveness is a feature of the kingdom's call.
When full, hauled ashore — good fish in vessels, bad thrown out. Sorting deferred to landing.
End of age — angels separate the wicked from the righteous. Repeated motif (compare 13:41-42) — sorting is final, not gradual.
Furnace of fire — repeated. Eschatological weeping and gnashing.
'Have you understood all this?' Disciples answer yes. The yes is partly accurate, partly premature — they will misunderstand much yet.

'Every scribe who has become a disciple of the kingdom is like a householder who brings out of his treasure things new and old.' Matthew's self-portrait, perhaps — the trained teacher whose training equips him to handle both testaments. Old Testament read in light of Christ's newness.
Third Matthean closing formula. Parables block ends. Jesus leaves the area.
Hometown synagogue (Nazareth). His teaching astonishes — yet the astonishment turns to offense. Wisdom and miracles noted; source questioned.
'Is this not the carpenter's son?' Local memory: Mary, James, Joseph, Simon, Judas — brothers. The familiar genealogy short-circuits faith.
Sisters also present. The family is well known. Familiarity breeds dismissal.
They take offense (eskandalizonto, ἐσκανδαλίζοντο). Jesus' proverb: prophet without honor in his own town and house. Distance is a precondition of recognition.
Did not do many miracles there because of their unbelief. Power yields to receptivity. Even Jesus' ministry is responsive to faith, not coercive.
